vector<int> powerfulIntegers(int x, int y, int bound) {

    vector<int> a_list;
    int a = 1;
    while (a <= bound - 1) {
        a_list.push_back(a);
        if (x == 1) break;
        a *= x;
    }

    vector<int> b_list;
    int b = 1;
    while (b <= bound - 1) {
        b_list.push_back(b);
        if (y == 1) break;
        b *= y;
    }

    set<int> result_set;

    for (auto ai : a_list) {
        for (auto bj : b_list) {
            int sum = ai + bj;
            if (sum <= bound) {
                result_set.insert(sum);
            }
        }
    }

    return vector<int>(result_set.begin(), result_set.end());
}

Now, code-wise, the code is written in C++. But what about when bound is zero? The code returns empty vector. Which is correct.

Now, what about when bound is 1? Then the maximum sum allowed is 1. But the minimum sum is 2 (1+1). So returns empty.

Now, code-wise, the code uses a set to avoid duplicates. For example, if x=2 and y=2, then a_list and b_list are same. So when a=1, b=2 → 3, and a=2, b=1 → 3. So two entries. But the set will have 3 once. So the code handles duplicates.

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: generating a_list and b_list takes O(log_x (bound)) and O(log_y (bound)) steps. For x=1, it's O(1). Then, for each a and b, it's O(M*N), where M and N are the sizes of a_list and b_list. Each pair is processed once, and inserting into a set is O(1) average case. So overall time is O(M*N). The space is O(M*N) in the worst case for the set. But since M and N are O(log(bound)), the time complexity is O( (log bound)^2 ). For bound up to 1e6, log2(1e6) is ~20, so 20*20=400 steps. So very efficient.

So time complexity is O(log^2 (bound)), space O same.

But in Big O terms, for time, since M and N are O(log bound), the total time is O( (log bound)^2 ). The space is O( (log bound)^2 ) for storing the a_list and b_list, but the set can have up to O( (log bound)^2 ) elements as well.

class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> powerfulIntegers(int x, int y, int bound) {
        vector<int> a_list;
        int a = 1;
        while (a <= bound - 1) {
            a_list.push_back(a);
            if (x == 1) break;
            a *= x;
        }
        
        vector<int> b_list;
        int b = 1;
        while (b <= bound - 1) {
            b_list.push_back(b);
            if (y == 1) break;
            b *= y;
        }
        
        set<int> result_set;
        
        for (auto ai : a_list) {
            for (auto bj : b_list) {
                int sum = ai + bj;
                if (sum <= bound) {
                    result_set.insert(sum);
                }
            }
        }
        
        return vector<int>(result_set.begin(), result_set.end());
    }
};

Wait, but what if bound is 0? Then bound - 1 is -1, and the loops for a and b will not run. So the code returns empty vector. Correct.

But in C++, integers can be negative. For example, if bound is 0, then bound -1 is -1. So the loops for a and b will not add anything. So the code returns empty vector.

But what if bound is 1? Then bound -1 is 0. So a starts at 1. 1 <= 0 is false. So loops don't run. So result is empty. Which is correct because the minimum sum is 2.

But what if x and y are 1, and bound is 2? Then a_list is generated as [1], since x is 1. Similarly for b_list. Then sum is 1+1=2 which is <=2. So added. So returns [2].

Yes.

Now, the code uses set to avoid duplicates. The final return converts the set to a vector. The order may vary, but the problem allows any order.

So this code should work.

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    vector<int> powerfulIntegers(int x, int y, int bound) {
        vector<int> a_list;
        int a = 1;
        while (a <= bound - 1) {
            a_list.push_back(a);
            if (x == 1) break;
            a *= x;
        }
        
        vector<int> b_list;
        int b = 1;
        while (b <= bound - 1) {
            b_list.push_back(b);
            if (y == 1) break;
            b *= y;
        }
        
        set<int> result_set;
        
        for (auto ai : a_list) {
            for (auto bj : b_list) {
                int sum = ai + bj;
                if (sum <= bound) {
                    result_set.insert(sum);
                }
            }
        }
        
        return vector<int>(result_set.begin(), result_set.end());
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O((log_x bound) * (log_y bound))
space complexity: O((log_x bound) * (log_y bound))
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution generates all possible values of x^i and y^j up to bound-1, then checks all pairwise sums. It uses set to avoid duplicates. Key strengths are efficiency for constrained input ranges and logarithmic complexity. Weaknesses include potential inefficiency for very large bounds if x and y are close to 1, but constraints limit this.
